title: Valuation of Exotic Options and Counterparty Games Based on Conditional Diffusion
summary: This paper addresses the challenges of pricing exotic options and structured products, which traditional models often fail to handle due to their inability to capture real-world market phenomena like fat-tailed distributions and volatility clustering. We introduce a Diffusion-Conditional Probability Model (DDPM) to generate more realistic price paths. Our method incorporates a composite loss function with financial-specific features, and we propose a P-Q dynamic game framework for evaluating the model's economic value through adversarial backtesting. Static validation shows our P-model effectively matches market mean and volatility. In dynamic games, it demonstrates significantly higher profitability than a traditional Monte Carlo-based model for European and Asian options. However, the model shows limitations in pricing products highly sensitive to extreme events, such as snowballs and accumulators, because it tends to underestimate tail risks. The study concludes that diffusion models hold significant potential for enhancing pricing accuracy, though further research is needed to improve their ability to model extreme market risks.
text:
Valuation of Exotic Options and Counterparty Games Based on Conditional
Diffusion — From Path Generation to Economic Value
P Trader: Conditional Diffusion Models for Exotic Option Valuation: From
Path Generation to Economic Value in a P-Q Game
Helin Zhao
Johns Hopkins University
Junchi Shen
University of Washington Seattle
Abstract— The pricing and trading of exotic options and structured products
have long been core challenges in financial markets. Traditional models such as
geometric Brownian motion and Monte Carlo simulations often fail to capture
the fat-tailed distributions and volatility clustering observed in real markets
due to their simplifying assumptions. This limitation has become particularly
evident in recent risk events involving products like “snowballs.” This paper
introduces the Diffusion-Conditional Probability Model (DDPM) into exotic
option pricing and designs a composite loss function incorporating volatility
clustering, tail risk, and drift constraints to generate price paths more aligned
with market behavior.
Methodologically, we propose an innovative P-Q dynamic game framework: the
P-model, representing a diffusion-based trader, engages in adversarial backtest-
ing against the Q-model (a GBM Monte Carlo-based market maker) to evaluate
the practical economic value of the generated model. Static validation reveals
that the P-model performs well in matching mean and volatility, aligning closely
with real market distributions, though it still falls short in kurtosis fitting.
Dynamic games further reveal its strengths and limitations: For European
and Asian options, the P-model demonstrates significantly higher profitability
than the Q-model, reflecting its precision in modeling terminal values and path
means. However, for lookback options and structured products like snowballs
and accumulators—which are highly sensitive to extreme events—the P-model
tends to incur losses due to underestimating tail risks.
This study demonstrates that diffusion models incorporating finance-specific loss
functions hold significant potential for generating realistic paths and enhancing
pricing accuracy for certain derivatives, though challenges remain in modeling
extreme risks. The proposed P-Q dynamic game framework provides an ef-
fective tool for evaluating the true economic value and potential shortcomings
of generative models in finance, laying a research foundation for future exten-
sions in risk-neutral calibration, multi-asset derivative modeling, and AI-driven
financial applications.
Keywords—Exotic options, structured products, diffusion models, Unet, option
pricing, Monte Carlo simulation, financial derivatives, neural networks
1
arXiv:2509.13374v1  [q-fin.PR]  16 Sep 2025
Introduction
Options and structured products, as pivotal financial derivatives, provide con-
tract holders with specific payoff structures based on the performance of un-
derlying assets at predetermined times and conditions. They serve as effective
tools for investment institutions to manage risk, hedge exposures, and optimize
investment portfolios.
With the continuous development of financial markets and the diversification of
investor demands, financial institutions have invented a wide variety of exotic
options based on the principles and experience of standard options. Exotic
options can be further categorized according to their complexity: relatively
simple exotic options such as Asian options, barrier options, lookback options,
and ratchet options primarily add a single feature to standard options; while
highly complex structured products like snowball products, phoenix notes, shark
fin options, and cumulative products feature multiple path-dependent conditions
and intricate payoff structures. These innovative financial instruments not only
broaden investor choices but also provide powerful tools for more refined and
personalized risk management and investment strategies[1].
Precisely because exotic options and structured products exhibit high levels of
diversity, customization, and structural complexity, accurate pricing remains a
core challenge for all market participants. For buyers, accurately assessing a
product's true value and potential risks is often diﬀicult. For sellers, pricing
errors can lead to significant financial losses. For regulators, complex product
structures make it challenging to establish unified, straightforward, and eﬀicient
modeling and evaluation mechanisms, often hindering timely intervention before
risk exposures materialize.
Taking cumulative warrants as an example, these products were widely sold in
Hong Kong and Asian markets during the 2000s. Their highly path-dependent
structure exposed extreme risks during periods of severe market volatility, even
earning the ironic nickname “I will kill you later” from the market. This term
was cited by mainstream media outlets such as The Wall Street Journal, reflect-
ing widespread concerns over severe losses and a crisis of confidence stemming
from misjudged risks.
A more recent case occurred in the Chinese mainland A-share market in 2024,
where a large number of investors suffered massive principal losses due to the
knock-in mechanism triggered during market declines while holding snowball
structured products (Autocallables). This sparked intense public outcry, involv-
ing billions of dollars. These challenges not only eroded investor confidence and
willingness to adopt exotic options and structured products but also hindered
the long-term development and institutionalization of this derivatives market.
Therefore, the industry has continuously introduced new technologies for exotic
options trading. For instance, as early as 2011, industry leader JPMorgan Chase
adopted NVIDIA GPUs to accelerate processing for over 50% of its financial
2
derivatives, successfully boosting computational speed by 40 times and enabling
calculations to be completed within minutes. Previously, such simulations often
required overnight processing. Similarly, in 2021, JPMorgan trained a “deep
hedging” neural network to hedge ratchet options, anticipating its deployment
within months. These industry practices demonstrate that financial institutions
consistently seek computational and modeling technologies beyond traditional
frameworks to address pricing challenges. This provides crucial industry context
and motivation for exploring next-generation, data-driven generative models for
options trading.
Although multiple commonly used pricing models for exotic options and struc-
tured products exist within the industry today, each has its limitations. From
the perspective of practicality and versatility, simulation methods—particularly
Monte Carlo simulation—represent the most cost-effective technical approach
currently available for pricing structured products and exotic options. This
method generates extensive paths of underlying asset prices through Brownian
motion simulations and, when combined with the product's payoff structure, can
be applied to price virtually any path-dependent or complex derivative instru-
ment. However, traditional Monte Carlo methods are built upon the assumption
of perfectly random asset price fluctuations, failing to adequately account for
the nonlinear characteristics and skewed distributions observed in real markets.
In recent years, the rapid advancement of deep learning technologies, partic-
ularly generative models, has provided novel approaches to addressing these
challenges. Such models not only possess robust path-generation capabilities
but also demonstrate unique advantages in financial time series modeling by
learning the latent structural features embedded within data distributions. This
exploration is not merely a matter of transplanting popular technologies into
financial applications; rather, it leverages their inherent alignment with the mod-
eling logic of financial markets themselves, offering a powerful complement to
traditional valuation and pricing methodologies.
Current mainstream derivative pricing methods, such as Monte Carlo simula-
tions, are largely based on the assumption that prices evolve over time through
a diffusion process. They often employ forward modeling of asset price paths
using stochastic processes like geometric Brownian motion (GBM). In contrast,
the modeling approach of diffusion models operates in the opposite direction. It
trains neural networks to progressively restore the data distribution disrupted
by noise by gradually injecting Gaussian noise into real price paths. This en-
ables the model to learn to generate market-style latent paths and distributions
in reverse. This process of stepwise denoising and recovering structure from
chaos mirrors the financial market's evolution where information continuously
flows in and prices gradually emerge from uncertainty. It excels at capturing
decisive market ‘styles’—such as fat-tailed risks and volatility clustering—that
traditional GBM models and other generative models struggle to characterize.
This aligns with the unique advantages diffusion models demonstrate in image,
video, and music domains.
3
After thorough training, diffusion models can learn the intrinsic structure and
characteristics of price paths from historical data. The paths they generate not
only capture the nonlinear behavior observed in real markets but also exhibit
a natural adaptability to specific underlying asset inputs. Therefore, compared
to Monte Carlo simulations centered on random sampling, diffusion models may
demonstrate superior expressive power in terms of the realism and flexibility of
path modeling.
To put it more vividly, the Monte Carlo model functions like a 3D printer,
continuously printing standardized components one by one at each time point
from start to finish based on predefined parameters. In contrast, the diffusion
model employs a “stepwise denoising” process akin to sculpting a “marble block”
composed of noise. Here, the neural network acts as a chisel, progressively
removing excess layers to ultimately reveal the price path as the final “artwork.”
If this approach can serve as a P-trading model for traders, it offers a novel
option valuation method and trading strategy. If the model can be calibrated
to risk neutrality and used as a Q-pricing model for market makers, it represents
a trainable pricing model that potentially outperforms traditional GBM Monte
Carlo simulations.
The main contributions of this paper are as follows:
• Introducing the generative diffusion model DDPM into the application
scenario of pricing exotic options;
• Constructing an additional loss function based on the characteristics of
financial paths to make the model's results more applicable to the financial
domain;
• Beyond traditional static comparisons, conducting a dynamic game eval-
uation with the P-model and Q-model as traders and market makers to
determine the economic benefits and evaluation results of the model in
financial applications.
• Proposing a technical framework to potentially enhance the accuracy of
derivative valuation and pricing, providing empirical support and practical
guidance for extending deep learning applications to complex financial
product modeling.
In summary, this paper aims to bridge the gap between traditional simulation-
based pricing and valuation techniques and the expressive capabilities of modern
generative neural networks. By modifying the loss function within the diffusion
model framework, we strive to provide a more realistic, accurate, and flexible
approach for the valuation and pricing of complex financial derivatives.
This paper primarily examines the perspective of exotic option trading parties,
specifically the design process of the P-model. It elaborates on the P-Q model
trading game evaluation method constructed based on the non-risk-neutral char-
acteristics of the P-model, extending beyond static valuation models. In sub-
4
sequent research, after completing the risk-neutral calibration of the diffusion
model, the authors will interpret the Q-pricing model from a market maker's
perspective and conduct related hedging evaluations.
Literature Review
Although this paper adopts the P model as the trader's perspective, the method
for obtaining product prices can still be understood from the Q model's view-
point. Just as the Q model design can draw inspiration for improvement from
the P model, both are fundamentally price regression methods based on obser-
vations and understanding of financial markets.
Structured Products and Exotic Option Pricing
For pricing structured products and exotic options, the industry widely em-
ploys four mainstream methodologies. First is the simulation approach, which
generates numerous potential future price paths for the underlying asset. By
combining these paths with the product's payoff structure, it calculates dis-
counted cash flows for each path and estimates fair value through statistical
averaging. This method is particularly suited for complex scenarios with strong
path dependency. Second is numerical integration. When the payoff function
and the underlying asset's probability distribution have analytical expressions,
this method directly integrates their product to eﬀiciently compute expected
returns. However, its applicability is constrained by model integrability and
expressibility. The third category involves decomposition strategies. This ap-
proach breaks down complex structured products into combinations of standard
options, bonds, and underlying exotic options, then prices each component using
established models. It is a common technique for products with clear structures.
Finally, the partial differential equation (PDE) method constructs differential
equations describing the dynamic relationship between asset prices and product
value. This provides a theoretical framework capable of capturing continuous
state transitions and the dynamic impact of market variables, though its solu-
tion complexity is high.[2]
Additionally, the Black-Scholes pricing model holds a prominent position in
option pricing. However, for structured products, its limitations are significant.
Due to the model's risk-neutrality assumption and single volatility parameter,
the Black-Scholes model struggles to effectively handle non-linear payoffs and
complex multi-layered structured products.
Within academia, research on option pricing methods has consistently been a
hot topic and has been increasingly innovative. After reviewing multiple papers,
we observe that throughout history, each period has seen improvements to the
aforementioned methods or the introduction of novel concepts for option pricing
research. Examples include ant colony optimization pricing [3] (2008), tensor
network methods [4] (2025) as improvements to standard Monte Carlo methods
5
(1977) [5], including Monte Carlo simulations based on the Merton model (1976)
[6], the Brownian bridge Monte Carlo simulation method (2008) [7], particle
swarm optimization Monte Carlo pricing (2021) [8], and direct neural network
approaches for structured option pricing such as KAN neural networks (2024) [9]
and LSTM neural networks [10], alongside other generative models like GAN
[11]. During our literature review, we also identified approaches for neural
network risk neutralization and transforming P-models into Q-models [12].
Diffusion model overview
The DDPM model finds its primary application in image generation models,
with its foundational paper being Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models [13].
Subsequently, as one of the early explorations applying diffusion models to time
series forecasting, TimeGrad [19] introduced a conditional generation frame-
work. Its core innovation lies in utilizing a recurrent neural network (RNN) to
encode historical sequences and external covariates, thereby generating a hidden
state. This hidden state then serves as a condition to guide generation during
each step of the diffusion model's reverse denoising process, enabling the model
to generate more reliable future sequences based on historical information. A
recent application of diffusion models to stock markets is DIFFSTOCK [14].
Neural Network
U-Net Model Architecture
The U-Net architecture was originally designed for medical image segmentation,
and its unique skip connections and encoder-decoder structure make it particu-
larly well-suited for processing sequential data [13]. In this study, we adopted
one-dimensional U-Net architecture to handle financial time series data. U-Net
employs a symmetric encoder-decoder architecture, specifically comprising: in
encoder section, with downsampling block, used to reduces sequence length via
max pooling while increasing feature dimensions; The residual block, where each
block contains two convolutional layers separated by batch normalization and
ReLU activation; The skip connection which used to reserves intermediate en-
coder features for subsequent decoding. As for decoder, with upsampling block,
used to restores sequence length via transposed convolution or interpolation,
feature Fusion part, which concatenates upsampled features with corresponding
encoder features. Lastly, Residual Block, which is similar residual structure to
the encoder, but with progressively reduced feature dimensions.
Temporal Embedding Mechanism
To address the temporal dependency of sequence data, the model incorporates
sine position encoding [7]:
6
𝑇 𝑖𝑚𝑒𝐸𝑚𝑏(𝑡) = [𝑠𝑖 (𝜔 1𝑡) , 𝑐𝑜 (𝜔 1𝑡) , … , 𝑠𝑖 (𝜔 𝑑𝑡) , 𝑐𝑜 (𝜔 𝑑𝑡) ]
Here,𝜔𝑖 = 1
10000
2𝑖
𝑑
. The frequency parameter is d, the embedding dimension.
This encoding method offers the following advantages: Absolute position aware-
ness: It provides the model with absolute position information for each time step.
Relative position awareness: Through the properties of trigonometric functions,
the model can learn relative temporal relationships. Extrapolation capability:
Sine functions exhibit strong extrapolation properties, enabling the model to
handle time steps not observed during training.
Conditional Embedding Network
The feature vector c undergoes nonlinear transformation through a multilayer
perceptron

Where 𝑊1 ∈ 𝑅 𝑑ℎ ×𝑑𝑐 ,𝑊2 ∈ 𝑅 𝑑𝑒 ×𝑑ℎ are weight matrices￿ 𝑏1 ∈ 𝑅 𝑑ℎ￿𝑏2 ∈

Feature Fusion Strategy
Time embeddings, conditional embeddings, and spatial features are fused
through a concatenation operation:
𝑓𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, 𝑐) = 𝑈 𝑁 𝑒𝑡 (𝑥𝑡 ⊕ 𝑇 𝑖𝑚𝑒𝐸𝑚𝑏(𝑡) ⊕ 𝐶𝑜𝑛𝑑𝐸𝑚𝑏(𝑐))
Functions
Each residual block adopts the following structure
𝑅𝑒𝑠𝐵𝑙𝑜𝑐𝑘(𝑥) = 𝑥 + 𝐶𝑜𝑛𝑣(𝑅𝑒𝐿𝑈 (𝐵𝑁 (𝐶𝑜𝑛𝑣(𝑥)))) where BN denotes batch nor-
malization and Conv represents the convolution operation. Residual connections
not only mitigate the vanishing gradient problem but also enhance the model's
training stability.
Injection points for conditional information in U-Net include Input layer, Condi-
tional embeddings are concatenated directly with input features; Intermediate
layers, Conditional information interacts with intermediate features via cross-
attention mechanisms; Output layer, Conditional information influences the gen-
eration of final features
7
Research Methods
In the Chinese stock market, we selected the CSI 1000 Index as our primary
research subject. As a stock index encompassing large-, mid-, and small-cap
stocks in China, it serves as a powerful research tool for studying the Chinese
stock market. It is also a commonly used underlying asset for over-the-counter
options and structured products in China. For both traders and market makers,
it is a widely researched and utilized stock index. Its broad market coverage
also provides greater research universality and practical liquidity for active par-
ticipation compared to individual stocks.
Regarding the selection of the risk-free rate, in China we follow the practice
of over-the-counter options market makers by using the Shanghai Interbank
Offered Rate (SHIBOR) as the benchmark rate and match the interest rate
according to the duration of the option contract.
After obtaining data from akshare covering the period from January 1, 2014, to
August 1, 2025, we employed sliding windows of 30 days, 90 days, 180 days, and
365 calendar days. These timeframes correspond to the risk-free interest rate
cycles of 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year. Daily sliding slices were
generated to produce historical price path data. The dataset was split into a
training set and an out-of-sample test set using January 1, 2024, as the cutoff
point. Strict forward-looking data prevention measures were applied to data
before and after the split to ensure the training model's effectiveness.
Ultimately, we obtained 8,023 path data points for model training and 827 test
data points for model evaluation and pricing games.
Data Preprocessings
Before sampling, we match the corresponding risk-free interest rate based on
the sliding window size and prevent potential interest rate data gaps through
front-filling, aligning by date. We then count the actual trading days within
the contract's trading period and calculate the historical volatility for the year
preceding each start date.
To enhance model training, we transform the actual price path into log-return
data, enabling the model to accurately reconstruct the path using the initial
price.
The following are the conditional parameters we used as inputs to the conditional
diffusion model.
Let 𝐶 be the set of all calendar days, and 𝑇 ⊂ 𝐶 be the set of all trading days.
We define two function to measure time between any two dates: 𝑠, 𝑡 ∈ 𝐶 .
Number of Calendar days: the function 𝐶𝐷 ∶ 𝐶 × 𝐶 → 𝑁 0, gives the number
of calendars days between 𝑠 and 𝑡 inclusive of the state of the start date 𝑠 but
exclusive of the end date 𝑡.
8
Number of Trading Days: the function 𝑇 𝐷 ∶ 𝐶 × 𝐶 → 𝑁 0, gives the number of
trading days between s and t inclusive.
For any 𝑠, 𝑡 ∈ 𝐶 , the number of trading days is always less than or equal to the
number of calendar days, i.e., 𝑇 𝐷(𝑠, 𝑡) ≤ 𝐶𝐷(𝑠, 𝑡).
We define the time to maturity: In Calendar Time to Maturity, for a given
maturity T, the calendar time to maturity is defined as 𝑇𝑐𝑎𝑙𝑒𝑛𝑑𝑎𝑟 = 𝐶𝐷(𝑠,𝑇 )

Model training
Forward process
The forward process of a diffusion model defines a parameterized
Markov chain, which gradually adds Gaussian noise to transform the
original data distribution 𝑝𝑑𝑎𝑡𝑎 (𝑥0)
into a standard normal distribution 𝑁 (0, 𝐼) .
Specifically, given the original data 𝑥0 ∈ 𝑅 𝐵 × 𝐶× 𝑇 ￿where 𝐵 is the batch size,
𝐶 is the number of channels, and 𝑇 is the sequence length, the forward process
can be expressed as:
𝑞 (𝑥𝑡|𝑥𝑡−1) = 𝑁 (𝑥 𝑡; √1 − 𝛽𝑡 ⋅ 𝑥𝑡−1, 𝛽𝑡 𝐼)
Here, 𝛽𝑡 ∈ (0, 1) is a predefined noise schedule parameter, and I is the identity
matrix. Through recursive expansion, for any time step 𝑡 ∈ 1, 2, … , 𝑇 �𝑥 𝑡 can
be sampled directly from 𝑥0:
𝑞 (𝑥𝑡| 𝑥 0) = 𝑁 (𝑥 𝑡; √𝛼𝑡 ⋅ 𝑥0, (1 − 𝛼𝑡) 𝐼)
Here 𝛼𝑡 = 1 − 𝛽 𝑡 represents the signal retention coeﬀicient, and 𝛼𝑡 = ∏
𝑡
𝑖 = 1 𝛼𝑖
represents the cumulative signal retention coeﬀicient.
Using the re-parameterization trick, 𝑥𝑡 can be expressed as 𝑥𝑡 = √𝛼𝑡 ⋅ 𝑥 0 +
√1 − 𝛼𝑡 ⋅ 𝜖𝑡, where 𝜖𝑡 ∼ 𝑁 (0, 𝐼) is an independently and identically distributed
Gaussian noise.
Reverse Diffusion Process
The reverse process is the core of the diffusion model, with the goal of gradually
restoring the original data distribution 𝑝𝑑𝑎𝑡𝑎 (𝑥0) from a noise distribution 𝑥𝑇 ∼
𝑁 (0, 𝐼). For the reverse process, this is a parameterized Markov chain, with each
step represented by a Gaussian distribution. We model the reverse process for
each step as 𝑝𝜃 (𝑥𝑡−1|𝑥𝑡)
According to Bayes' theorem, the reverse transition probability can be expressed
as: 𝑝𝜃 (𝑥𝑡) = 𝑞(𝑥𝑡−1)⋅𝑝𝜃(𝑥𝑡−1)
𝑝𝜃(𝑥𝑡) , where 𝑞 ( 𝑥 𝑡−1) is the known forward transition
probability, and 𝑝𝜃 (𝑥𝑡−1) and 𝑝𝜃 (𝑥𝑡) are the prior distributions for the reverse
10
process. However, directly calculating this posterior distribution is computa-
tionally infeasible because 𝑝𝜃 (𝑥𝑡) involves complex integrals.
To overcome computational diﬀiculties, this research adopts a variational ap-
proximation method. Specifically, we assume the reverse transition distribution
follows a Gaussian distribution: 𝑝𝜃 (𝑥𝑡) = 𝑁 (𝑥 𝑡−1; 𝜇𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡) , Σ 𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡))
Here 𝜇𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡) and Σ𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡) are the mean and covariance functions parameter-
ized by a neural network. This parameterization strategy not only maintains
mathematical simplicity but also ensures the numerical stability of the reverse
process.
Conditional Reverse Process
The conditional reverse process is the core of the conditional gen-
eration mechanism. Its transition distribution can be expressed as:
𝑝𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑐) = 𝑁 (𝑥 𝑡−1; 𝜇𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, 𝑐) , Σ 𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, 𝑐))
Here, 𝜇𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, 𝑐) and Σ𝜃 (𝑥𝑡, 𝑡, 𝑐) are functions parameterized by a neural net-
work. They depend not only on the current state 𝑥𝑡and time step 𝑡 ,but also
explicitly on the conditional information 𝑐.
Total Loss Function Definition
We employ a composite loss function to train the denoising diffusion probabilistic
model, ensuring that the generated sequences are not only consistent with the
real data in content but also capture its unique statistical properties. Our total
loss function L_total is a weighted sum, comprising a core mean squared error
loss and multiple auxiliary regularization loss terms, as shown in the formula.

Relative Jump Loss (𝐿𝑗𝑢𝑚𝑝)
To enable the model to accurately simulate the sharp fluctuations
between adjacent time steps in the data (like price gaps in financial
markets), we introduce the relative jump loss. This loss calculates
the L1 distance between the differences of adjacent elements in the
predicted sequence and the true sequence, which helps the model
capture the local dynamics of the sequence.

V olatility clustering loss ( 𝐿𝑣𝑜𝑙 𝑐𝑙𝑢𝑠𝑡 )
V olatility clustering is a key characteristic of time series, indicating
that periods of high volatility and low volatility tend to cluster to-
gether. W e measure local volatility by dividing the sequence into
overlapping windows and calculating the standard deviation within
each window. The volatility clustering loss measures the difference
between the predicted sequence and the true sequence in these local
volatilities:

Quantile Pinball Loss ( 𝜆𝑝𝑖𝑛𝑏𝑎𝑙𝑙 )
T o capture the Comparing to MSE, quantile loss (Pinball) assesses
how well a model predicts a specific percentile of the outcome. [7].
In finance, particularly in the stock market, there is a heavy-tailed
effect. This makes the standard Mean Squared Error (MSE), which
only captures the average path, less effective. Therefore, we use the
Pinball loss function, which is primarily designed to capture two crit-
ical scenarios under extreme market conditions, thereby building a
dynamic, asymmetrical framework for assessing risk and opportunity .
𝐿𝑞 (𝑦, ̂ 𝑦) = { 𝑞 ⋅ (𝑦 − ̂ 𝑦) , 𝑦 ≥ ̂ 𝑦
(1 − 𝑞) (𝑦 − ̂ 𝑦) , 𝑦 < ̂ 𝑦
14
Let ￿ be the target quantile and y be the real data, y ̂ is the estimated
data. When using 𝜏 = 0.01, When using a high quantile (e.g., 𝜏
=0.99), the model can apply a huge penalty (weight of 0.99) when
the predicted value is greater than the actual value (i.e., when the
predicted loss is not high enough). In contrast, a very small penalty
(weight of 0.01) is applied when the predicted value is less than the
actual value (i.e., when the predicted loss is too high). This forces the
model to be extremely conservative and focus on outlining the bottom
line of risk; When using a low quantile (e.g., 𝜏 =0.01), the model will
severely penalize cases where the predicted value is less than the
actual value (i.e., missing out on a huge gain) with a weight of 0.99,
while largely ignoring cases where the predicted value is greater than
the actual value with a weight of 0.01.
By combining these two approaches, we can better capture the two-
sided data patterns in financial markets. This is a loss function de-
signed to reinforce the accuracy of predictions for extreme risks and
events. Such a loss function design allows the model to better exhibit
the heavy-tailed characteristic of financial markets.
spectral loss ( 𝐿𝑠𝑝𝑒𝑐𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑙 𝑙𝑜𝑠𝑠 )

DDIM Price Path Generation
To meet the demands for speed and accuracy when generating financial data,
we use the DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models) model instead of the
DDPM model. DDIM is a deterministic non-Markovian chain with a determin-
istic sampling process, which allows for control over the randomness.
Unlike the DDPM model, where each step is a random sample and must be exe-
cuted sequentially for T steps, DDIM's sampling process allows for a ”jumping”
or ”skipping” style, where you can choose any arbitrary step k (𝑘 ≪ 𝑇 ) . Here,
T refers to the total number of diffusion steps in the DDPM model.
Assuming a reverse process , 𝑃 (𝑥 𝑡−1|𝑥𝑡, 𝑥0) satisfies the following normal distri-
bution:
𝑃 (𝑥 𝑡−1|𝑥𝑡, 𝑥0) ∼ 𝑁 (𝑘𝑥 0 + 𝑚𝑥𝑡, 𝜎2)
𝑥𝑡−1 = 𝑘𝑥 0 + 𝑚𝑥𝑡 + 𝜎𝜖, 𝜖 ∼ 𝑁 (0, 1)
W e have:
𝑥𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑣 = √𝛼𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑣( 𝑥𝑡 − √1 − 𝛼𝑡𝜖𝑡√𝛼𝑡
) + √1 − 𝛼𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑣 − 𝜎2𝜖𝑡 + 𝜎 2𝜖
where 𝑥𝑡 and 𝑥𝑝𝑟𝑒𝑣 can be multiple iterative steps.
Model Path Evaluation
This section quantitatively evaluates the paths generated by our proposed con-
ditional diffusion model on a sample test set. We will perform a comprehensive
statistical evaluation of the paths generated from multiple different starting
points. The mean and standard deviation (std) of various metrics are sum-
marized in Table 5.1 to evaluate the average performance and stability of the
model.
I. model route generation result
mean std
Mean Difference 0.0053 0.0037
Volatility Difference 0.1137 0.09217
Kurtosis Difference 3.559 3.517
KS Statistic 0.199 0.094
16
mean std
KS P-value 0.2087 0.2407
Wasserstein Distance 0.00836 0.00505
QQ R-squared 0.889 0.0518
Based on the quantitative results in Table 5.1, we can conduct an in-depth
analysis of the model's performance from the following perspectives:
The model excels at capturing the core statistical features of market paths.
• Mean and Overall Distribution Shape: The mean of the Mean Differ-
ence is only 0.0053, indicating that the model can accurately learn the
expected drift term from historical data. More importantly, the mean of
the Wasserstein Distance is extremely low (0.00836), which signifies that
the ”actual distance” between the model-generated distribution and the
real distribution is minuscule.
• Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) Test Results: The average p-value of the KS
test is 0.2087, a value far greater than the 0.05 significance level. This
suggests that, statistically, we cannot reject the null hypothesis that the
generated data and the real data come from the same distribution. There-
fore, the overall distribution of the paths generated by the model passes
this rigorous statistical test, proving the model's validity.
• The model demonstrates a good fit for volatility and quantiles.
The model performs well in matching volatility and quantiles, although the
results also show some variability.
• Volatility Matching: The mean of the Volatility Difference is 0.1137, show-
ing that the model can generally match the market's risk level well. How-
ever, its standard deviation (0.09217) is relatively large, implying that
the model's ability to capture volatility might have some deviation under
certain specific market conditions.
• Quantile-Quantile Relationship: The mean of the QQ R-squared reaches
0.889, which is a considerably high value, confirming a strong linear corre-
spondence between the quantiles of the generated and real distributions.
The challenge of capturing fat-tail characteristics is worthy of further research.
This evaluation also clearly reveals the main challenge the model currently faces:
insuﬀicient ability to capture extreme market events (i.e., fat-tail characteris-
tics).Significant Kurtosis Difference: The mean of the Kurtosis Difference is as
high as 3.559, the most significant discrepancy among all metrics. This indicates
that even though we incorporated specialized training for fat-tail properties in
the loss function, the tails of the distributions from the model-generated paths
are still ”thinner” than those of the real market, failing to fully replicate the
extreme risks commonly found in financial time series.Interpretation of Other
17
Metrics: The discrepancy in kurtosis also explains why the QQ R-squared does
not perfectly approach 1.0; its fitting deviation primarily comes from both ends
of the distribution (i.e., the tails).
In summary, the conditional diffusion model we propose demonstrates powerful
overall capabilities and immense application potential in path generation tasks.
It can accurately replicate the market's mean, volatility, and overall distribution
shape, achieving satisfactory results on several key metrics. However, there is
still a noticeable gap in the model's ability to fully capture the ”fat-tail” nature
of extreme events. This does not negate the overall value of the model but
rather points us to key directions for future optimization. For instance, we
could explore introducing more advanced network architectures, designing loss
functions that are more sensitive to tail data, or conducting longer training
periods to further enhance the model's capacity to characterize market tail risk.
Trader–Market-Maker Dynamic Quoting Game
As a P-measure trading model, the P model, especially from a research-trader’s
standpoint—naturally invites expectations about out-of-sample trading perfor-
mance. To test it, we pair the P model against a risk-neutral Q model acting
as the counterparty in backtests.
18
Game framework
W e implement the Q side with a geometric Brownian motion (GBM)
Monte Carlo pricer. This choice serves two purposes: (i) P and
Q receive identical inputs, enabling clean head-to-head comparisons;
and (ii) GBM-based Monte Carlo remains a canonical risk-neutral
pricing approach that is straightforward to construct.
T o mitigate limitations of the baseline Q pricer, we evaluate the mar-
ket maker under varying profit targets (hereafter, a greediness pa-
rameter). A higher greed level gives the Q side more room to quote
with margin and to profit after hedging, even when its model is im-
perfect. T rade is executed only when the relative price gap between
P and Q for the same contract exceeds 10%—a threshold intended to
cover transaction costs and filter weak signals.
V olatility clustering is a key characteristic of time series, indicating
that periods of high volatility and low volatility tend to cluster to-
gether. W e measure local volatility by dividing the sequence into
overlapping windows and calculating the standard deviation within
each window. The volatility clustering loss measures the difference
between the predicted sequence and the true sequence in these local
volatilities:
Product set
We consider five contract types:
• European options. Although not exotic, Europeans are the most comfort-
able setting for the Q (GBM Monte Carlo) model and serve to evaluate
the P model’s accuracy on terminal prices.
• Lookback options. The payoff references the path maximum (e.g., the
highest asset price over trading days within the contract period). This
product tests how well the diffusion model captures extreme values over
the horizon.
• Asian options. The payoff depends on the average price over the contract
period. This tests the diffusion model’s fidelity in modeling path averages.
• Accumulator (structured product). The contract specifies a discount
(strike) level and a knock-out level. If knock-out is hit, the product is
terminated early. Prior to expiry/KO, the buyer must purchase the
underlying daily at the discount price when spot is below that level,
and 2× daily notional at the discount price when spot is below the
discount (with all purchases accruing to payoff accounting), with final
settlement at maturity. Accumulators sparked intense debate in East
Asia (Hong Kong, Taiwan) and were dubbed “I will kill you later” in
19
popular commentary. Pricing this product probes day-by-day path
accuracy; strong performance would be especially valuable for investors,
market makers, and regulators.
• Snowball (autocallable) notes. A more complex structured product and a
stress test for the model’s holistic capability. It features both a knock-out
(KO) barrier and a knock-in (KI) barrier. If the price touches/exceeds
KO on any observation date, the note autocalls with principal plus a
predetermined high coupon. If KI is breached during the life and the note
does not KO by expiry, the investor’s principal becomes fully exposed to
downside, producing equity-like loss. If the path remains between KI and
KO until maturity, the investor typically receives a preset coupon (often
with principal protection). Snowball valuation is highly sensitive to the full
path distribution and thus to tail events (KI), upside potential (KO), and
volatility clustering, which affects barrier-touch probabilities over time.
Given the product’s prominence and recent risk events in China, superior
valuation by the P model here would have significant practical relevance.

infer which side holds the positional edge. The worst performer is the contract
with discount 80% and 𝐾𝑂 =150%. Its hallmark is rare 𝐾𝑂 (hard to auto-
call) combined with the largest investor concession (very deep discount). This
mix is deceptively attractive—it appears to offer wider profit room—but typi-
cally leads to longer time-in-trade, during which distant price moves can erase
early gains and “kill you later.” In short, lower discounts and higher 𝐾𝑂 levels
make accumulators more misleading for buyers, consistent with the product’s
reputation.

On the product that places the highest demands on path realism, we obtain a
more nuanced picture. Because the generator under-learns extreme events and
heavy tails, it tends to underestimate deep drawdowns and—under that belief—
over-sell snowball notes. When the KI is set at 90% of S0, risk is partly masked
by the milder terms, and the model appears to manage exposures reasonably.
However, with a deeper KI at 80%, the model incorrectly infers “more room”
and shifts toward selling rather than buying; realized paths show that a single
severe downturn can flip many trades from profit to large losses. This aligns
with our broader diagnosis: tail underestimation is a primary failure mode for
P on snowballs.
Conclusion
This study, to our knowledge, is among the first to introduce DDPM-style dif-
fusion generators to exotic-option pricing and to evaluate them through a P–Q
dynamic game with an adversarial, economics-of-trading lens. The diffusion
26
model functions as a useful evaluator for traders (P model) and performs partic-
ularly well on Asian options, where path-average fidelity matters most. Never-
theless, the model requires stronger learning of heavy tails and extreme events,
as evidenced by lookbacks, accumulators, and especially snowballs.
Outlook
We will (i) pursue risk-neutral calibration to obtain a trainable Q (pricing)
model suitable for market-making; (ii) explore adversarial co-training between
the Q pricer and the P trader; and (iii) extend to multi-underlying systems that
model cross-asset dependence. The latter will enable valuation and trading of
multi-asset exotics—such as rainbow and basket options—via multi-underlying
slices rather than single-path slices, bringing the framework closer to real capital-
market settings.
